_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to use Newton's laws of motion to quantitatively analyse how force, mass and acceleration are related.

_source.w: We are learning to use Newton's laws of motion to quantitatively analyse how force, mass and acceleration are related.
y: Newton's laws provide a precise, mathematical framework for predicting and explaining the motion of objects — from sports balls to spacecraft — making them among the most practically useful ideas in all of physics.
Learning to apply F = ma quantitatively develops our ability to translate physical situations into mathematical models, a core skill in physics, engineering and many STEM careers.
These laws, formulated over 300 years ago, remain accurate for everyday situations and demonstrate how a small number of fundamental principles can explain an enormous range of phenomena.
eg: Applying Newton's second law (F = ma) to calculate the acceleration of a 1200 kg car when a net force of 3600 N is applied.
Investigating Newton's first law experimentally: demonstrating that an object at rest remains at rest (and an object in motion remains in motion) unless acted on by an unbalanced force.
Using Newton's third law to explain why a rocket accelerates — the exhaust gases are pushed backward (action), and the rocket is pushed forward (reaction) with equal and opposite force.
Solving multi-step problems involving friction: calculating the net force on an object by subtracting frictional force from applied force, then determining the resulting acceleration.
Analysing the forces on a person in a lift — explaining why they feel heavier when accelerating upward and lighter when accelerating downward, using free-body diagrams and F = ma.
Investigating the relationship between mass and acceleration experimentally using a dynamics trolley and pulley system, and comparing measured results with predictions from Newton's second law.
